Participating within a DIY space

Main concept

The main concept of a DIY space is self-organization and sharing skills and information. This means that everyone that participates in a DIY space is responsible for how that space is organized with the other people in that space and that everyone brings something to the proverbial table. In context to the events Big Fat Pers organize, this could mean that you bring along some vegan snacks for the snack table, that you help with setting up the space, that you help with cleaning up the space, that you help others in doing the same, facilitating workshops or skill shares and much, much more. The question is what you can bring to enhance the space/event not what you can get out of the space/event or what the space/event can give you.

Contribution vs consumption

As Big Fat Pers we are aware of how we live in a society that is based on consumption, with money being it’s currency. The more money you have the less you yourself can choose to contribute to society.

In a consumer space people come to an event (often) having paid for the event in advance. They do their thing within the parameters of the event, pay for their drinks and leave at a designated time expecting others to clean up after them. You might be someone who brings their glass back to the bar, throws away their own trash and/or cleans up the things they themselves have used i.e. leaving everything as they found it. The vibe is quite simple and clear, pay for using the space within the context of the event and go when expected.

In a DIY/contributor space it is less straight forward. Firstly, people come to an event knowing what the minimum donation is and are aware of their own financial capability’s and strive to donate more than asked. They do this because they know that the minimum donation asked is aimed under the range of covering costs. The space does this to make the event more accessible to everyone, instead of it being aimed at making a profit and in doing so excluding other people.

During the event people are aware of what capabilities they bring to the event. For example, someone who is a chatter box can contribute by making the extra effort to talk to those who seem shy or to help explain how the space works. Someone who has energy for ten could go around the space now and then to clean the used cups that have been abandoned. Another can share information with others around the subject the event is aimed at and so on and so forth.

Before someone leaves a DIY event, they don’t only make sure they paid their tab and have cleaned up after themselves. They look around at what still needs to be done or ask what they can do within their own capabilities and restrictions. Someone that needs to leave early can’t be expected to help put everything away while the event is still in progress but can contribute none the less. It is up to the individual to decide how to do so.

What you can do to contribute to a space is very personal in nature and the variations are only limited by our own creativity. There is no way to name them all here. We hope these examples will help you think about how you contribute to a DIY space instead of being a consumer. It is not about leaving the space as you found it but adding to the space and the people in it.

If you would like to collaborate or contribute, please contact us by e-mail bigfatpervs@riseup.net
